name: PyDESeq2 (Differential Expression for Bulk RNA-seq)

performance.notes: NumPy/SciPy; typical bulk RNA-seq datasets (100-1000 samples) are fast

agent_readiness.notes: DESeq2 differential expression analysis in Python. from pydeseq2.dds import DeseqDataSet. from pydeseq2.ds import DeseqStats. dds = DeseqDataSet(counts=counts_df, metadata=metadata_df, design_factors='condition'). dds.deseq2(). stat_res = DeseqStats(dds, contrast=['condition', 'treated', 'untreated']). stat_res.summary(). results_df = stat_res.results_df. Shrinkage: stat_res.lfc_shrink(). p_adj. log2FoldChange. baseMean. Compatible with DESeq2 R output format. Handles size factors and dispersion estimation.

gotchas: Python reimplementation — minor numerical differences from R DESeq2
Requires raw count matrix (not normalized) as input
Small sample sizes (<3 per group) produce unreliable estimates
LFC shrinkage (apeglm equivalent) uses different method than R
best_when: Bulk RNA-seq differential expression analysis in pure Python workflows
When R DESeq2 cannot be used but DESeq2 methodology is required
avoid_when: If R is available (use original DESeq2 R package for maximum accuracy)
Single-cell data (use scvi-tools or Scanpy's rank_genes_groups)
Very small experiments (<3 replicates per condition)
summary: PyDESeq2 is a Python reimplementation of DESeq2 for bulk RNA-seq differential expression. DeseqDataSet(counts, metadata, design_factors='condition').deseq2(). DeseqStats(dds, contrast=[...]).summary(). results_df with log2FoldChange, p_adj. LFC shrinkage. MIT by Owkin. Best when R is unavailable but DESeq2 methodology needed. Requires raw counts. Minor numerical differences from R DESeq2.
